default.summary: A steady yoga posture that should feel supported, spacious, and calm when practised with control.
steps: Set your base first and move into the shape slowly instead of forcing the final position.
Lengthen your spine before trying to go deeper so the pose comes from alignment, not strain.
Relax your jaw, face, and shoulders while keeping the main working muscles active.
Hold for several smooth breaths and leave the pose as carefully as you entered it.
alignment_tips: Distribute weight evenly through your main contact points.
Keep the neck long and avoid collapsing into the lower back.
Use a smaller range of motion if your breath becomes choppy or you feel pinching.
Choose control and steadiness over depth.
default.breathing: Inhale to create length and space. Exhale to settle into the pose without forcing it.
default.warnings: Back out of the pose if you feel sharp pain, numbness, dizziness, or joint compression. Use props or a smaller shape when needed.

families.name: grounded_seated
families.summary: A grounded seated or kneeling pose that builds posture, calm focus, and steady breath.
steps: Sit on a folded blanket or block if that helps you lengthen the spine comfortably.
Place your legs and feet deliberately so both sides of the pelvis feel balanced on the floor.
Lift through the crown of the head and soften the ribs so the lower back stays neutral.
Rest your hands lightly, relax the shoulders, and stay for several even breaths.
alignment_tips: Root evenly through the sitting bones or shins.
Keep the chest broad without thrusting the ribs forward.
If the knees sit very high, add support under the thighs or hips.
Choose height under the seat before rounding the spine.
families.breathing: Take smooth nasal breaths and let each exhale release unnecessary effort in the face, jaw, and shoulders.
families.warnings: Support the hips or knees if your joints feel compressed. Avoid forcing the legs into position.

families.name: restorative_recovery
families.summary: A restorative posture designed to release excess tension and make breathing feel slower and easier.
steps: Set up the pose so your neck, lower back, and hips feel supported before you settle in.
Use props or reduce the range of motion until the body can soften without guarding.
Let the front body relax while keeping enough structure to protect the joints.
Stay still for several calm breaths and come out gradually.
alignment_tips: Support tight areas instead of pushing through them.
Keep the throat soft and the face relaxed.
Let the breath widen the ribs and belly without strain.
If the lower back arches too much, reduce the depth or add support.
families.breathing: Lengthen the exhale and allow each inhale to expand the ribs, side body, and back body gently.
families.warnings: Use props generously. Exit early if you feel tingling, pinching, or any pressure in the knees, neck, or lower back.

families.name: cat_cow_spinal_wave
families.summary: A spinal mobility pattern that links breath to movement and teaches healthy articulation through the back.
steps: Start on hands and knees with wrists under shoulders and knees under hips.
On an inhale, lengthen the front body and broaden the collarbones without collapsing the low back.
On an exhale, press the floor away and round from the upper back to the tailbone.
Move slowly for several cycles and make the wave smooth rather than dramatic.
alignment_tips: Keep the elbows straight but not locked.
Spread the fingers and press evenly through both hands.
Let the pelvis initiate part of the movement instead of only moving the neck.
Keep the range comfortable if the wrists or knees are sensitive.
families.breathing: Inhale for extension, exhale for rounding, and make the breath set the pace.
families.warnings: Pad the knees if needed and keep the motion gentle if you have acute back pain.

families.name: foundational_strength
families.summary: A strength-based foundation pose that asks for long lines, active shoulders, and intelligent weight distribution.
steps: Establish your hands or feet first so the base feels stable and even.
Create length from the crown of the head through the spine before deepening the shape.
Engage the core and shoulders so the torso stays supported rather than sinking.
Hold with smooth breaths and come out before alignment starts to unravel.
alignment_tips: Press evenly through the whole hand or foot, not only one corner.
Draw the lower ribs inward to support the trunk.
Keep the neck in line with the spine instead of dropping the head heavily.
Bend the knees or shorten the stance if the hamstrings or shoulders limit length.
benefits: Lengthens the back line of the body
families.breathing: Use steady nasal breathing and keep the exhale strong enough to support the core.
families.warnings: Protect the wrists, shoulders, and hamstrings by reducing load or range when needed.

families.name: lunges_warriors
families.summary: A dynamic standing family that develops grounded legs, open hips, and confident posture.
steps: Place the feet first so your stance is stable from heel to toes.
Bend or hinge only after you feel the legs actively supporting the pelvis.
Lengthen through the spine and reach through the arms without tightening the neck.
Stay steady for several breaths, then exit with the same control you used to enter.
alignment_tips: Track the front knee in the same direction as the toes.
Keep the back leg active instead of letting it go passive.
Square or open the hips only as much as the pose requires, not more.
Use a shorter stance if balance or breath is unstable.
families.breathing: Inhale to lengthen the spine and arms. Exhale to root through the legs and steady the pelvis.
families.warnings: Adjust depth if the front knee, lower back, or hip flexors feel strained.

families.name: standing_balance_side_body
families.summary: A standing balance or side-body posture that depends on a steady base and clear directional reach.
steps: Ground through the standing foot or lower support points before lifting or extending.
Lengthen both sides of the waist so the spine stays spacious.
Reach through the top arm or lifted leg without gripping the jaw or shoulders.
Fix your gaze on one point and hold only as long as your breath remains even.
alignment_tips: Micro-bend the standing knee if locking the joint throws you off balance.
Press down to grow upward instead of leaning into the lower back.
Stack shoulders and hips as much as the pose asks for without twisting unintentionally.
Use a wall or shorten the range when balance is inconsistent.
families.breathing: Breathe quietly and keep the exhale smooth so balance comes from steadiness, not tension.
families.warnings: Practise near a wall if needed and avoid forcing the lifted leg higher than you can stabilise.

families.name: twists_and_revolves
families.summary: A twisting posture that should begin with spinal length and only then move into rotation.
steps: Set the feet or seat firmly so the pelvis has a stable base.
Inhale to lengthen the spine upward before beginning the twist.
Exhale to rotate from the ribs and upper spine while keeping the lower back long.
Stay broad across the chest and unwind slowly after several breaths.
alignment_tips: Grow taller before trying to twist deeper.
Keep both sides of the waist long rather than crunching one side.
Anchor the base so the twist comes from the trunk instead of yanking with the arms.
If the breath feels blocked, reduce the depth.
families.breathing: Use the inhale to create length and the exhale to refine rotation without force.
families.warnings: Avoid cranking into the lower back. Keep the twist gentle if you have disc or SI joint issues.

families.name: forward_folds_openers
families.summary: A deep fold or opener that asks for patience, support, and gradual length through the spine and hips.
steps: Enter the pose slowly and elevate the hips or use props before chasing maximum depth.
Lengthen the spine first so the movement comes from the hips rather than collapsing the torso.
Keep the active leg or grounding points engaged to protect the joints.
Hold only where the breath stays calm and the stretch feels broad, not sharp.
alignment_tips: Lead with a long spine rather than a rounded back.
Support tight hips or hamstrings with props and a smaller range.
Keep the knees and ankles comfortable instead of forcing them flat.
Let time and breath create depth.
families.breathing: Breathe into the back body and soften more on each exhale without collapsing.
families.warnings: Back off immediately from any sharp sensation in the knees, hamstrings, or lower back.

families.name: backbends_heart_openers
families.summary: A heart-opening backbend that needs strong legs, active glutes, and length through the front body.
steps: Set your legs, hands, or forearms first so the base can support the backbend.
Lift the chest and lengthen through the sternum before trying to go deeper.
Engage the legs and lower belly so the bend spreads through the whole spine.
Leave the pose before the lower back takes all the load.
alignment_tips: Think of length first, depth second.
Keep the front ribs integrated so you do not hinge only at the lumbar spine.
Press through the legs to help the chest rise.
Broaden the collarbones and keep the throat spacious.
families.breathing: Inhale to lift and widen the chest. Exhale to stabilise the core and avoid overreaching.
families.warnings: Use moderation with sensitive lower backs, shoulders, or necks. Warm up thoroughly before advanced backbends.

families.name: inversions
families.summary: An inversion that demands shoulder stability, core control, and careful entry and exit.
steps: Practise your setup first and only invert when the shoulders and trunk feel organised.
Move into the shape with a light, controlled lift rather than a hard kick or collapse.
Stack the major joints as much as your mobility allows and keep the core engaged.
Come down with control before balance or neck position starts to fail.
alignment_tips: Press strongly through the forearms, shoulders, or hands depending on the pose.
Keep the neck neutral and avoid dumping weight into the cervical spine.
Use the ribs and pelvis to keep the midline organised.
Practise at a wall until the shape is truly stable.
families.breathing: Keep the breath smooth and conservative. If the breath becomes strained, exit the pose.
families.warnings: Avoid unsupported inversions if you have neck issues, uncontrolled blood pressure, or do not yet have a stable base.

families.name: arm_balances_core
families.summary: An arm balance that combines active hands, core engagement, and precise weight shift.
steps: Warm the wrists, shoulders, and core before attempting the full shape.
Place the hands firmly and spread the fingers to create a broad, active base.
Shift weight gradually while drawing the lower belly in to keep the centre controlled.
Lift only as high as you can maintain clean alignment and a steady breath.
alignment_tips: Look slightly forward instead of dropping the head.
Push the floor away so the shoulders stay active.
Keep the elbows tracking intelligently rather than collapsing outward.
Practise entry drills and toe taps before trying to hold the full pose.
families.breathing: Keep the breath short but smooth. Use each exhale to connect the ribs and lower belly.
families.warnings: Practise carefully if you have wrist, elbow, or shoulder pain. Use props or regressions first.

Adho Mukha Svanasana.summary: A classic downward-facing pose that lengthens the spine, opens the shoulders, and lightly energises the whole body.
steps: Start on hands and knees with hands shoulder-width apart and knees under hips.
Tuck the toes and lift the knees, sending the hips up and back.
Press the floor away, lengthen both sides of the waist, and keep a soft bend in the knees if needed.
Let the head hang naturally between the arms and stay for 5 to 8 smooth breaths.
alignment_tips: Prioritise a long spine over straight legs.
Press through the index-finger base and thumb mound to protect the wrists.
Rotate the upper arms outward slightly so the shoulders feel broad.
Keep the ribs gently drawn in so the back does not sag.
common_mistakes: Rounding the upper back instead of creating a long spine.
Putting all weight into the hands rather than pressing back through the hips.
Forcing the heels to the floor before the spine is long.
Dropping the head heavily — let it hang naturally between the arms.
Adho Mukha Svanasana.breathing: Inhale to create length from wrists to hips. Exhale to root the hands and settle the heels toward the floor.
Adho Mukha Svanasana.warnings: Keep the knees bent or elevate the hands if the hamstrings or shoulders feel overly tight.

Ardha Chandrasana.summary: Half Moon Pose challenges balance while opening the chest and strengthening the standing leg.
steps: Begin from Triangle or a short lunge with one hand on the floor or a block in front of the standing foot.
Shift weight into the front foot and lift the back leg until it reaches hip height.
Stack the top hip over the bottom hip and open the chest to the side.
Reach the top arm upward, keep the gaze steady, and hold for several breaths before lowering slowly.
alignment_tips: Use a block so the side body can stay long.
Keep the standing knee softly engaged, not locked.
Flex the lifted foot and press through the heel.
Think of length in both directions instead of hanging into the lower hand.
benefits: Opens the chest and side body
Ardha Chandrasana.breathing: Breathe slowly and let the exhale steady the standing leg and pelvis.
Ardha Chandrasana.warnings: Practise near a wall if balance is inconsistent or if you feel unstable in the standing ankle.

Janu Sirsasana.summary: Head-to-Knee Forward Fold is a calming asymmetrical fold that opens the hamstrings, calves, and back body.
steps: Sit with one leg extended and the other foot placed comfortably near the inner thigh.
Square the torso toward the straight leg and lift through the spine.
Hinge from the hips as you reach forward, keeping the chest broad.
Rest where the breath stays smooth and hold without pulling aggressively.
alignment_tips: Sit on height if the pelvis rolls backward.
Lead with the sternum instead of the forehead.
Keep a slight bend in the straight knee if the hamstring is tight.
Avoid twisting away from the extended leg.
Janu Sirsasana.breathing: Inhale to lengthen. Exhale to soften the torso forward without forcing the stretch.
Janu Sirsasana.warnings: Back off if you feel pulling behind the knee or compression in the lower back.

Savasana.summary: Final relaxation pose allows the nervous system to settle so the effects of practice can be absorbed.
steps: Lie on your back with the legs comfortably apart and the arms resting slightly away from the body.
Support the knees, head, or neck if that helps the body fully relax.
Close the eyes and let the whole body release into the floor.
Stay still for several minutes and come out by deepening the breath before rolling to one side.
alignment_tips: Position the shoulders so the chest feels open, not pinched.
Let the feet roll out naturally.
Use support under the knees if the lower back feels tense.
Keep the face and tongue soft.
Savasana.breathing: Allow the breath to become natural, quiet, and effortless. Observe rather than control it.
Savasana.warnings: If lying flat is uncomfortable, place support under the knees or choose a side-lying version.

Sukhasana.summary: Easy Pose is a simple cross-legged seat for grounding, meditation, and breath awareness.
steps: Sit on a folded blanket so the knees can relax downward more easily.
Cross the shins comfortably and place the hands on the thighs or knees.
Lift through the spine and soften the shoulders down the back.
Stay upright for several slow breaths and switch the cross of the legs over time.
alignment_tips: Raise the seat until the spine can stay tall without strain.
Let the knees be supported if they float high.
Keep the chin level and the back of the neck long.
Avoid slumping or leaning back heavily.
common_mistakes: Slumping the lower back and collapsing the chest.
Forcing the knees down instead of supporting them with height under the hips.
Clenching the jaw or shoulders during the hold.
Keeping the same leg crossing every session — alternate regularly.
Sukhasana.breathing: Use slow nasal breathing and let the exhale soften the hips and groins.
Sukhasana.warnings: If the knees or hips feel strained, sit higher or choose another seated shape.

Uttana Shishosana.summary: Extended Puppy Pose opens the chest, shoulders, and upper back while keeping the hips over the knees.
steps: Start on hands and knees with hips stacked over the knees.
Walk the hands forward and melt the chest toward the floor.
Keep the hips above the knees as the arms lengthen forward.
Rest the forehead or chin only if the neck stays comfortable and breathe into the upper back.
alignment_tips: Keep the lower ribs gently supported so the low back does not dump.
Press evenly through the palms and forearms of the hands.
Let the chest move forward rather than only downward.
Use a blanket under the knees for comfort.
Uttana Shishosana.breathing: Direct the inhale into the side ribs and upper back, then soften the sternum on the exhale.
Uttana Shishosana.warnings: Come up slightly if the shoulders or neck feel pinched.

Vajrasana.summary: Thunderbolt Pose is a kneeling seat often used for breathing practice, digestion support, and quiet focus.
steps: Kneel with the shins on the floor and sit the hips back toward the heels.
Place the hands on the thighs and lengthen the spine upward.
Keep the chest relaxed and the shoulders soft while the pelvis feels heavy.
Stay for calm breaths, then come out slowly if the feet or knees need relief.
alignment_tips: Place a block or cushion between the heels and hips if needed.
Keep the ankles long and the toes pointing back comfortably.
Draw the lower ribs in lightly to avoid overarching the low back.
Balance weight evenly over both shins.
Vajrasana.breathing: Breathe softly through the nose and let the exhale settle the thighs and pelvis.
Vajrasana.warnings: Use support or shorten the hold if the knees, ankles, or tops of the feet feel strained.

Virabhadrasana I.summary: Warrior I builds grounded strength through the legs while opening the front body and lifting the chest.
steps: From a staggered stance, turn the back foot slightly outward and bend the front knee.
Square the torso toward the front as much as your hips comfortably allow.
Lift the arms overhead while lengthening through both sides of the waist.
Press firmly through both feet and hold with steady breath.
alignment_tips: Track the front knee toward the second toe.
Keep the back heel rooted or narrow the stance if balance is difficult.
Draw the front ribs in so the lower back stays long.
Relax the shoulders away from the ears.
Virabhadrasana I.breathing: Inhale to lift the torso and arms. Exhale to root through both feet and stabilise the pelvis.
Virabhadrasana I.warnings: Shorten the stance if the lower back or front knee feels overloaded.

Virabhadrasana II.summary: Warrior II strengthens the legs and hips while teaching broad, stable expansion through the arms and chest.
steps: Step the feet wide and turn the front foot out with the back foot angled slightly in.
Bend the front knee until it stacks over the ankle while keeping the back leg strong.
Extend the arms out at shoulder height and broaden across the collarbones.
Gaze over the front fingertips and hold with a steady, even breath.
alignment_tips: Keep the front knee tracking over the toes.
Anchor the outer edge of the back foot.
Stack the shoulders over the pelvis instead of leaning forward.
Soften the neck and jaw.
Virabhadrasana II.breathing: Inhale to widen through the arms. Exhale to deepen your root through both feet.
Virabhadrasana II.warnings: Reduce the depth if the front knee or inner groin feels strained.

Virabhadrasana III.summary: Warrior III is a strong balance that links leg power, hip control, and long spinal alignment.
steps: Begin from a lunge or Warrior I and shift your weight into the front foot.
Lift the back leg as the torso tips forward until both move toward one long line.
Reach through the crown of the head and the lifted heel.
Keep the standing leg steady and hold only as long as the hips stay level.
alignment_tips: Square the lifted hip down rather than opening it to the side.
Keep the standing knee softly engaged.
Draw the lower belly in to support balance.
Use fingertips on a wall or block if needed.
Virabhadrasana III.breathing: Keep the breath light and steady so balance stays organised rather than rigid.
Virabhadrasana III.warnings: Use support if you feel unstable or if the standing hamstring is overworking.

Vriksasana.summary: Tree Pose is a grounding balance posture that trains single-leg stability, a tall spine, and quiet focused attention.
steps: Stand tall in Mountain Pose and shift your weight evenly into the right foot.
Lift the left foot and place the sole against the inner thigh or calf — never against the knee joint.
Bring the palms together at the chest or raise the arms overhead with relaxed shoulders.
Fix your gaze on one still point, breathe steadily, then switch sides.
alignment_tips: Press the standing foot evenly and actively into the floor.
Keep the standing knee softly engaged rather than locked.
Draw the tailbone gently down so the lower back stays long.
Relax the shoulders away from the ears and keep the chin level.
common_mistakes: Placing the lifted foot against the knee — always use the calf or inner thigh.
Leaning the torso away from the standing side.
Gripping the face and jaw instead of staying mentally calm.
Over-arching the lower back by lifting the ribs away from the pelvis.
benefits: Opens the hip of the lifted leg
Vriksasana.breathing: Breathe quietly through the nose. Let the exhale deepen your root down through the standing foot.
Vriksasana.warnings: Use a wall for support if balance is inconsistent, or keep the raised foot lower on the calf.
